zh.Birstall, Yorkshire, England: 英國英格蘭越可爾比爾斯塔爾
zh.lifeSummary: Sir John John Galmyngton Roger Boys John Beaumount Thomas de Wyllyngton William Brythlegh John Poulet Thomas de Wyllyngton John Beaumont於1495年出生在英國英格蘭越可爾比爾斯塔爾。他於1500年10月15日與Jane Beaumont在英國英格蘭越可爾結婚。他們至少是1個兒子的父母。他去世於1560年在英國英格蘭戴文享年65歲。

zh-hans.Birstall, Yorkshire, England: 英国英格兰越可爾比爾斯塔爾
zh-hans.lifeSummary: Sir John John Galmyngton Roger Boys John Beaumount Thomas de Wyllyngton William Brythlegh John Poulet Thomas de Wyllyngton John Beaumont于1495年出生在英国英格兰越可爾比爾斯塔爾。他于1500年10月15日与Jane Beaumont在英国英格兰越可爾结婚。他们至少是1个儿子的父母。他去世于1560年在英国英格兰戴文享年65岁。

nameMeanings.name: Beaumont
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Andre, Gilles, Gaetan, Honore, Jacques, Jean-Guy, Marcel.

English (of Norman origin) and French: habitational name from any of the five places in Normandy or several others elsewhere in France so named. The placename comes from Old French beu, bel ‘fair, lovely’ + mont ‘hill’. There are also places in England so named under Norman influence in Cumberland, Lancashire, and Essex, the last of which changed its name in the 12th century from Fulepet ‘foul pit’ to Bealmont ‘beautiful hill’; these may also have given rise to the surname. The surname is now widespread throughout England, but most common in Yorkshire. Compare Belmont .

History: Many English bearers of this surname are descended from Henry de Beaumont (died 1340), who came to England from France as a soldier, serving Kings Edward I, II, and III of England. He acquired the earldom of Buchan in Scotland by marriage. — Many American bearers of this surname are descendants of John Beaumont (1612–47), who came to North America from England in 1630. William Beaumont came from England to Cambridge MA in 1635 and subsequently moved to CT.

variants.name: Bement
meanings: English (Cambridgeshire): variant of   Beaumont  . This surname is now rare in Britain.

variants.name: Beeman
meanings: English (Kent): occupational name for a beekeeper, from Middle English bee-man ‘bee-keeper’ (Old English bēo + mann).
English (Kent): variant of   Beaumont  .
Americanized form of German Biemann or of its Dutch cognate Bieman.

variants.name: Belmont
meanings: French and English (of Norman origin): variant of   Beaumont  .
Catalan: habitational name from the placename Bellmont ‘beautiful mountain’. Compare Spanish   Belmonte  .

variants.name: Baumunk
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Siegmund.
German: probably a Germanized form of a French Huguenot name Beaumont (see also Beaumont ).

variants.name: Bayman
meanings: English (mainly Lancashire): variant of   Beaumont  .
Americanized form of German Beumann or Bäumann, variants of Baumann .

nameMeanings.name: Sir
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: ‘light; beauty; prosperity’, also ‘rank, power, royal majesty’, a byname of the goddesses Lakshmi and Saraswati. Also widely used as an honorific title.

nameMeanings.name: John
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: New Testament: ‘God is gracious’. See John .

nameMeanings.name: John

meanings: New Testament: ‘God is gracious’. See John .

nameMeanings.name: Roger
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: From an Old French personal name, Rog(i)er, of Germanic (Frankish) origin, from hrōd ‘fame’ + gār, gēr ‘spear’. This was adopted by the Normans and introduced by them to Britain, replacing the native Old English form Hrōðgār. Roger was one of the most popular boys' names throughout the medieval period, but less so after the Reformation, though it has continued in regular use to the present day. Roger, Count of Sicily ( c.1031–1101 ), son of Tancred, recovered Sicily from the Arabs. His son, also called Roger, ruled Sicily as king, presiding over a court noted for its splendour and patronage of the arts.

variants.name: Rodger
meanings: Variant spelling of   Roger  , in part from the surname derived from the given name in the Middle Ages.

nameMeanings.name: Boys
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Transferred use of the common Irish and Scottish surname, which is of uncertain derivation. It may originally have been a local name from the island of Bute in the Firth of Clyde, known in Gaelic as Bod (genitive case Bóid). The first known bearer of the surname is Robertus de Boyd, recorded in Scotland in the early 13th century.

nameMeanings.name: John
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: New Testament: ‘God is gracious’. See John .

nameMeanings.name: Beaumount
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Andre, Gilles, Gaetan, Honore, Jacques, Jean-Guy, Marcel.

English (of Norman origin) and French: habitational name from any of the five places in Normandy or several others elsewhere in France so named. The placename comes from Old French beu, bel ‘fair, lovely’ + mont ‘hill’. There are also places in England so named under Norman influence in Cumberland, Lancashire, and Essex, the last of which changed its name in the 12th century from Fulepet ‘foul pit’ to Bealmont ‘beautiful hill’; these may also have given rise to the surname. The surname is now widespread throughout England, but most common in Yorkshire. Compare Belmont .

History: Many English bearers of this surname are descended from Henry de Beaumont (died 1340), who came to England from France as a soldier, serving Kings Edward I, II, and III of England. He acquired the earldom of Buchan in Scotland by marriage. — Many American bearers of this surname are descendants of John Beaumont (1612–47), who came to North America from England in 1630. William Beaumont came from England to Cambridge MA in 1635 and subsequently moved to CT.

nameMeanings.name: Thomas
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: New Testament name, borne by one of Christ's twelve apostles, referred to as ‘Thomas, called Didymus’ (John 11:16; 20:24). Didymos is the Greek word for ‘twin’, and the name is the Greek form of an Aramaic byname meaning ‘twin’. The given name has always been popular throughout Christendom, in part because St Thomas's doubts have made him seem a very human character.

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Pet form of any of the given names beginning with the letter D- ( compare Jay and Kay ), in particular Dorothy . It is also used as an independent name, and may in some cases be associated with the River Dee ( compare Clyde ).

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

nameMeanings.name: John
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: New Testament: ‘God is gracious’. See John .

nameMeanings.name: Poulet
meanings: A diminutive of Paul.

nameMeanings.name: Thomas
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: New Testament name, borne by one of Christ's twelve apostles, referred to as ‘Thomas, called Didymus’ (John 11:16; 20:24). Didymos is the Greek word for ‘twin’, and the name is the Greek form of an Aramaic byname meaning ‘twin’. The given name has always been popular throughout Christendom, in part because St Thomas's doubts have made him seem a very human character.

en.lifeSummary: Sir John John Galmyngton Roger Boys John Beaumount Thomas de Wyllyngton William Brythlegh John Poulet Thomas de Wyllyngton John Beaumont was born in 1495, in Birstall, Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom. He married Jane Beaumont on 15 October 1500, in Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 1 son. He died in 1560, in Devon, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 65.

fr.lifeSummary: Sir John John Galmyngton Roger Boys John Beaumount Thomas de Wyllyngton William Brythlegh John Poulet Thomas de Wyllyngton John Beaumont est né en 1495, à Birstall, Everwyck, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni. Il a épousé Jane Beaumont le 15 octobre 1500, à Everwyck, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni. Ils ont eu au moins 1 fils. Il est décédé en 1560, à Devon, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, à l’âge de 65 ans.
